backlog.title: fix: install.py path resolves in consumer repos
backlog.description: SKILL.md routes install operations to `python3 skill/hedl/scripts/install.py`. In this (self-hosted) repo `skill/hedl/` is at the root so the path resolves. In a consumer repo, the skill is symlinked to `.claude/skills/hedl/` and `install.py` is NOT projected by any tier in `tiers.json`. An agent in an installed consumer repo following SKILL.md verbatim will hit 'file not found' for every install operation: --status, --doctor, --tier change, --migrate. Fix options (TBD at implementation time): (a) project `install.py` under one of the tiers, (b) re-route SKILL.md to the symlinked skill location, (c) ship a thin shim that resolves the skill path. This finding came from an external review with consumer context; a self-hosted /repo-health run cannot detect it because the failing condition does not exist in this repo.
acceptance_criteria: Install management instructions in SKILL.md resolve and execute successfully in an installed consumer repo (any tier).
A test simulates the consumer layout (skill symlinked to .claude/skills/hedl/) and exercises --status, --doctor, --tier change, and --migrate without 'file not found'.
am_i_done.py passes

backlog.title: fix: budget_manager does not create .work/ in gate-only tier
backlog.description: skill/hedl/scripts/budget_manager.py unconditionally calls `path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)` at lines ~118 and ~150. Gate-only adopters have no `.work/` by design — they install `--tier gate` precisely because they do not want the phase-tracking machinery. A budget operation silently creating `.work/` violates the tier contract and may confuse adopters into believing their tier is mis-configured. Invisible to a self-hosted /repo-health because this repo is team-tier and `.work/` exists from install. Fix: budget operations must detect the tier and either skip work-tracking writes on gate-only, route to a different location, or no-op cleanly. The exact resolution is part of the implementation.
acceptance_criteria: Running any budget_manager.py operation in a gate-only installed repo does not create `.work/` (or any subdirectory of it).
Tier-aware behaviour is covered by a test that installs `--tier gate` into a temp dir, runs the relevant budget operations, and asserts `.work/` is absent.
Existing team-tier behaviour of budget_manager is unchanged.
am_i_done.py passes

backlog.title: fix: versioning.md schema_version claim matches actual code
backlog.description: docs/versioning.md (around lines 69-70 and 108-118) claims that Hedl reads `schema_version` from `hedl.toml` under `[hedl] schema_version`. The actual code: install.py reads `schema_version` only from `.work/context.json` (see install.py:47-85); am_i_done.py reads `hedl.toml` only for `[verify]` (see am_i_done.py:151-152, 170-196). Neither reads `[hedl] schema_version` from hedl.toml. Either implement the `hedl.toml` read (treat the doc as the spec) or correct the doc to describe what the code actually does. Discovered via external review; self-hosted /repo-health cannot spot this because there is no spec-vs-code consistency check in the gate.
acceptance_criteria: Documentation in docs/versioning.md matches code behaviour exactly — either the read is implemented or the doc is corrected.
If the read is implemented: install.py and/or am_i_done.py read `[hedl] schema_version` from hedl.toml with documented precedence relative to `.work/context.json`, covered by tests.
If the doc is corrected: `change_class` is `docs` instead of `fix`; the doc states clearly where schema_version is stored and read from.
am_i_done.py passes

backlog.title: docs: retrospective Prior-art audit of ADR-001 through ADR-016
backlog.description: ADR-017 requires every ADR to include a `Prior art` section that names overlapping standards/practices and (if overlap exists) justifies Hedl's version. ADR-001 through ADR-016 were written before ADR-017 and do not include this section. Audit each one and add a Prior art section: name the overlapping practice (or state none), answer the three justification questions where overlap exists. Several of the early ADRs apply standard patterns (e.g. ADR-002 tiered adoption, ADR-007 naming convention, ADR-009 opinionated defaults) and should explicitly acknowledge this — being honest about not inventing is the point. ADR-017 does not block on this audit; ADR-001..016 remain Accepted but become more defensible with annotation.
acceptance_criteria: Each of ADR-001 through ADR-016 includes a `Prior art` section that either names overlapping standards/practices or states that no relevant prior art exists.

For ADRs with overlap: the section answers (a) what is genuinely different about Hedl's version, (b) why the difference is worth the cost, (c) what would have to change for Hedl to delegate to the existing thing.
am_i_done.py passes.

backlog.title: feat: promote existential-challenger to a named agent and add to dispatch-rules
backlog.description: ADR-017 requires `existential-challenger` to be a mandatory reviewer for any `.work/decisions/*.md` write. Currently `existential-challenger` exists only as a prompt in `skill/hedl/references/review-library.md`; the gate's config check expects mandatory agents to be named agents in `.claude/agents/`. Mirror the `determinism-auditor` promotion pattern: create `.claude/agents/existential-challenger.md` from the library prompt, remove the duplicate from review-library.md (library drops from 19 to 18 prompts), add `existential-challenger` to `.work/config/dispatch-rules.json` as mandatory for `.work/decisions/**` and `docs/alternatives.md` paths, and update all counts that reference '7 named agents' / '19 composable prompts' to '8' / '18' across README, tiers.md, docs/agents.md, and the generated SKILL.md section.
acceptance_criteria: `.claude/agents/existential-challenger.md` exists with valid frontmatter (mirror format of determinism-auditor).
The duplicate prompt is removed from review-library.md (library count drops to 18).
`existential-challenger` is in dispatch-rules.json as mandatory for `.work/decisions/**` and `docs/alternatives.md`.
All count references updated to '8 named agents' / '18 composable prompts' across README, tiers.md, docs/agents.md, generated SKILL.md.
am_i_done.py passes including the config check.

backlog.title: feat: lightweight-tier multi-operator support (assignees, claim/release, flock)
backlog.description: Per ADR-018, lightweight tier must support multi-operator coordination via local `.work/` mechanisms. Add an `assignee` field to work items, change `meta.active_item` to `meta.active_items` keyed by assignee, wrap `.work/work.json` writes with `flock` (mirror the budget_manager.py pattern), and add a claim/release lifecycle (open → claimed → in_progress → released → completed). Bump `schema_version` and add a migration. Solo adoption must be unchanged — assignee defaults to the current operator.
acceptance_criteria: Work items carry an `assignee` field (defaults to current git user if unset).
`meta.active_items` keyed by assignee replaces singular `meta.active_item`; backward-compat read is supported.
Concurrent writes to `.work/work.json` are flock-serialised (mirroring budget_manager).
Claim/release lifecycle: `/claim <id>` and `/release <id>` flows defined in references/commands.md.
`schema_version` increments; install.py migration handles the upgrade idempotently with state archival.
Solo operator workflow is functionally unchanged (no new prompts, no extra steps).
am_i_done.py passes.

backlog.title: feat: audit and harden team-tier GitHub Issues multi-operator integration
backlog.description: Per ADR-018, team tier inherits multi-operator semantics from the chosen state backend (GitHub Issues today). Audit the existing github-issues backend in install.py / am_i_done.py: does Hedl read GitHub assignees correctly? How are concurrent claims handled? Does the gate detect file-level conflicts across PRs from different operators? Fix or document any gaps found.
acceptance_criteria: Audit report committed (e.g. as a /repo-health-style review).
GitHub assignees are read into Hedl's view of work-item state.
Two operators claiming the same issue is detected and surfaced.
File-level conflict across operator PRs is caught by `--streams` (verify with a test).
Gaps are either fixed or documented as separate backlog items with clear scope.
am_i_done.py passes.

backlog.title: feat: tier-transition coordination-state migrations in install.py
backlog.description: Per ADR-018, tier transitions preserve coordination state. install.py migration registry must handle lightweight↔team transitions for assignees and claims: lightweight→team maps local assignees to GitHub Issue assignees; team→lightweight pulls GitHub assignees back to local. Both directions are idempotent and archive prior state before overwriting.
acceptance_criteria: install.py --tier <new> handles assignee/claim migration in both lightweight↔team directions.
Pre-migration state is archived to `.work/archive/<timestamp>/` (mirroring existing migration archival pattern).
Migration is idempotent: re-running --tier same is a no-op.
Tests cover both upgrade and downgrade paths with assignee state present.
am_i_done.py passes.

backlog.title: docs: update Principle 4 in CLAUDE.md from 'one work item at a time' to 'one per operator'
backlog.description: Per ADR-018, Principle 4 in CLAUDE.md (`One work item at a time — finish and validate before starting the next.`) is clarified as **one per operator**, not one per repo. Update the wording. Light docs change; bundle with the lightweight-multi-op implementation (WORK-0006) or stand alone.
acceptance_criteria: CLAUDE.md Principle 4 reads 'One work item per operator at a time'.
Any other reference docs that quote the principle (SKILL.md, references/) are updated consistently.
am_i_done.py passes.
